name: What happens if workers comp denies my claim for the object that hit me?
description: You have the right to appeal a denial through a formal hearing. You will need to present medical evidence and witness testimony to contest the insurance company

mainEntity.name: What happens if workers comp denies my claim for the object that hit me?

acceptedAnswer.text: You have the right to appeal a denial through a formal hearing. You will need to present medical evidence and witness testimony to contest the insurance company's reasons for the denial. An attorney is highly recommended for this stage.
acceptedAnswer.description:
If a falling object injury claim is denied, you have the right to an appeal, which usually involves a hearing before an Administrative Law Judge. Common reasons for denial include "pre-existing conditions," "no proof of workplace incident," or "injury not severe enough." The denial letter will state the reason and provide a deadline for filing your appeal.

During the appeal, you can present new evidence, such as independent medical exams or witness testimonies that verify the object fell and struck you. This is a formal legal proceeding that functions like a mini-trial.

Key factors include the specific legal basis for the denial and the strength of your medical evidence. Winning an appeal requires a deep understanding of the state's specific workers' comp statutes. Most workers who win their appeals do so because they hired an attorney to handle the complex filings and cross-examination of the insurance company's "hired-gun" doctors. Advice from an attorney before the hearing is critical.
